name: Propaganda Due P2
description: Propaganda Due (P2) was a clandestine, pseudo-Masonic lodge operating in Italy from the late 1970s until its exposure in 1981. Officially a Masonic lodge under the Grand Orient of Italy, it was secretly operating outside Masonic rules, engaging in political and financial intrigue. Led by Licio Gelli, P2 became a shadow government accused of undermining Italian democracy. Proponents claim P2 was involved in numerous acts of terrorism, assassinations, and financial crimes, aiming to destabilize Italy and establish an authoritarian regime. They point to the membership list, which included high-ranking military officers, politicians, journalists, and business leaders, as evidence of a vast conspiracy. Mainstream perspectives acknowledge P2's illegal activities and its attempt to influence Italian politics. However, the extent of its influence and its direct involvement in specific acts of violence remains debated. Many historians and political analysts see P2 as a symptom of the deep corruption and political instability that plagued Italy during the Years of Lead, rather than the sole cause of these problems. The degree to which P2 acted independently or served as a tool for foreign powers (such as the CIA's alleged involvement in Operation Gladio) is also a subject of ongoing debate. The lodge's exposure led to a major political scandal, the collapse of the government, and further investigations into Italian corruption.
subtitle: Italian Masonic Lodge Conspiracy

claims.evidence: Some P2 members were linked to neo-fascist groups implicated in the bombing. Gelli's involvement in other suspicious activities.
claims.criticism: While P2 members had connections to extremist groups, direct evidence linking P2 as an organization to the planning and execution of the Bologna bombing is lacking. The investigation remains controversial.

claims.claim: P2 was instrumental in covering up the Ustica Massacre (1980) plane crash.
claims.evidence: P2 members held positions in the Italian Air Force and intelligence agencies. Some documents suggest a cover-up.
claims.criticism: The Ustica Massacre remains unsolved, and while P2 members may have been involved in obstructing the investigation, their direct role in causing the crash is unproven. Multiple theories exist.

claims.claim: P2 aimed to destabilize Italy and establish an authoritarian government.
claims.evidence: Gelli's known fascist sympathies. The membership list included individuals with a vested interest in undermining democratic institutions. The lodge's accumulation of power and influence.
claims.criticism: While P2 clearly sought to influence Italian politics, whether its ultimate goal was a full-blown authoritarian takeover is debatable. Its actions may have been driven more by personal gain and power than by a coherent ideological agenda.

keyFigures.name: Tina Anselmi
keyFigures.role: Head of the Parliamentary Commission investigating P2

keyFigures.name: Roberto Calvi
keyFigures.role: Banker associated with P2, found dead in London

mythologyConnections: The idea of secret societies controlling world events

timeline.event: P2 transforms into a clandestine organization under Gelli's leadership
P2 is exposed following a police raid on Gelli's villa in Arezzo
The Italian Parliament declares P2 a criminal organization
Licio Gelli dies, never fully brought to justice

sources.author: Philip Willan
sources.work: Puppetmasters: The Political Use of Terrorism in Italy
sources.title: Philip Willan
